Let $n$ be the number of children we choose. We want to find the smallest $n$ such that we are guaranteed to have at least one pencil of each of the $4$ colors.
We can use the Pigeonhole Principle. We have $4$ colors (pigeonholes) and we want to find the smallest $n$ such that at least one color has at least $\lceil \frac{n}{4} \rceil$ pencils.
We want to find the smallest $n$ such that we are guaranteed to have at least one pencil of each color.
We can use the worst-case scenario. We could pick all the pencils of 3 colors first. Since there are 6 pencils of each color, we can pick all 6 pencils of 3 colors. This would be $3 \times 6 = 18$ pencils.
Then, the next pencil we pick must be of the remaining color. So, we need to pick $18 + 1 = 19$ pencils to guarantee that we have at least one pencil of each color.

Alternatively, we can use the generalized Pigeonhole Principle. We have 4 colors, and we want to find the smallest $n$ such that at least one color has at least $\lceil \frac{n}{4} \rceil$ pencils.

Another way to think about this is to use the formula for the minimum number of draws to guarantee at least $k$ items of a certain type from $n$ types, which is $n(k-1) + 1$.
